Require the Municipal Engineer to provide the GSO and the Inventory Committee with complete description and cost segregation of road components for road projects to facilitate the preparation of the RPCLRN;
Direct the GSO to prepare and submit the Report on Local Road Network, in accordance with the prescribed formats as provided under COA Circular No. 2015-008; and
Require the Municipal Accountant to disclose the total road network in the Notes to the FS, provide depreciation to all road networks and make adjusting entries regarding depreciation and derecognition of the replaced assets following the provisions of COA Circular No. 2015-008.

We recommended that Management:
Submit to the Audit Team copies of the MOA and supporting documents on the subject FTs and ensure their submission for any subsequent FTs; and
Direct the Accountant to:
Review, correct and update the identified Schedules with negative balance and conduct periodical check for negative abnormal balances in the accounts and adjust accordingly;
Evaluate the non-moving sub-account balances and facilitate the necessary liquidation/refund of unexpended balance of those projects to the SA;
Submit to the concerned SA the liquidation reports and return/remit the outstanding balance, for completed projects, if warranted.
If the disposition of the unspent/unutilized amount is not provided in the MOA, Trust Agreement or Memorandum of Understanding, the agency may, at their option, utilize the said amount by sending a letter to the SA requesting approval to transfer the unexpended balance to the GF (Section 3.2 of COA Circular 2012-001); and
Reconcile the Due to NGAs account and take up the necessary correcting/adjusting entries for fair presentation in the FS.

We recommended that Management require:
The Municipal Accountant to:
Facilitate the immediate transfer of the completed projects to its respective appropriate PPE accounts in accordance with Section 50 of the Manual on NGAS;
Work back to trace the entries of the dormant CIP accounts and ascertain the status of the projects recorded;
Make the necessary adjustments to correct the actual balance of the CIP accounts, PPE accounts, the corresponding Depreciation Expense accounts and other related accounts affected by the non-transfer of completed projects and errors that could have been committed in the recording of the CIP; and
Recognize CIP based on progress billings and other acceptable and appropriate supporting documents which will properly substantiate its balance.
The Municipal Engineer to constantly update the Municipal Accountant on the status of accomplishments of on-going Municipality’s projects for accurate presentation of related accounts in the FS.

We recommended the Management to require the AOs to have all collections as of year-end be remitted and deposited intact and in full amount with the Authorized Government Depository Bank (AGDB) and to reconcile the abnormal balances identified in the Cash in Local Treasury Accounts and effect the necessary adjusting/correcting entries to present fairly balances in the FS. Henceforth, strict adherence to Section 69 of PD No. 1445 and Sections 31 and 32 of Manual on NGAS for LGUs is required.

We recommended that the Agency Head instruct the Municipal Accountant to immediately submit the lacking/unsubmitted CY 2023 TBs, Financial Reports and Statements, DVs with complete supporting documents, collections accounts and other monthly reports as well as the lacking DVs from prior years. Otherwise, the subject disbursements will be disallowed in audit.

Henceforth, require the Municipal Officials concerned to comply with the rules and regulations on the submission of paid Disbursement Vouchers, Official Receipts, Journal Entry Vouchers, FS and other Financial Reports.

We recommended that Management strictly comply with DILG, DOF and DBM JMC No. 1, as follows:
Maximize the utilization of the 20 percent DF and implement the programs, projects and activities within the targeted timeframe to achieve the social, economic and environmental outcomes essential in the attainment of the constitutional objective of a desired quality of life for all;
Reprogram and reallocate resources as soon as the need arises to ensure that the Municipality is maximizing its available resources to achieve the expected results it has committed; and
Formulate well-defined procedures to enhance the monitoring and evaluation activities during project implementation, which includes among others, the coordination with the Municipal Engineer in monitoring the implementation of projects funded from the 20 percent DF to attain the optimal socio-economic development and environmental management outcomes which is the core of the fund allocation.

We recommended that Management:
Strictly adhere to the above cited provisions on the procurement of infrastructure projects. Henceforth, all perfected contracts together with its supporting documents must be submitted to the Office of the Auditor within five working days from execution of contract for the required auditorial review and technical evaluation; and
Direct concerned officials to submit lacking documents and the BAC Secretariat to maintain a checklist of required documents to be submitted and ensure that contracts for infrastructure projects is properly supported with complete documentation.

We recommended that Management require:
The Property Officer to prepare and submit the required PIF which contains the listing of all insurable properties and other assets of the agency to the Audit Team and of the GSIS GIF;
Official concerned to ensure that all insurable properties and other assets are adequately covered/insured with the GIF, administered by the GSIS to adequately and reliably protect against any damage to or loss of its properties or assets and interest due to fortuitous events/casualty; and
Refrain from securing insurance coverage from a private insurance company unless such property or part thereof is not acceptable to the GSIS GIF.

We recommended that Management:
Inform the Audit Team within 10 days after the award of the infrastructure project or before the start of the program/activity that the appropriate project signboards and/or public notices are already posted for the validation of the Audit Team; and
Require the Municipal Engineer to provide the Audit Team, at the beginning of the year, with a list of all on-going government PPAs and those that are to be implemented during the year including the required data/information as well as the monthly monitoring report on the Project Status (in Annex “A”) in accordance with Sections 2, 3.1 and 3.2 of COA Circular No. 2013-004.

We recommended that Management:
Direct the GFPS to fully implement the GAD PPAs and monitor their implementation to ensure that optimum benefits are derived by its constituents; and
Direct the GFPS and GAD key personnel to undergo capacity building programs and training on gender and development to enhance their knowledge and build their competencies on GAD-related laws and international commitments, gender mainstreaming, gender analysis, gender-responsive planning and budgeting, and GAD-related tools like HGDG on the attributed budget to evaluate gender responsiveness of PPAs in the GAD Plan and Budget.

We recommended that Management require:
The Municipal Accountant to:
Facilitate the immediate transfer of the completed project to its appropriate PPE account in accordance with Section 50 of the Manual on NGAS;
Work back to trace the entries of the dormant CIP accounts and ascertain the status of the projects recorded;
Make the necessary adjustments to correct the actual balance of the CIP accounts, PPE accounts, the corresponding Depreciation Expense accounts and other related accounts affected by the non-transfer of completed projects and errors that could have been committed in the recording of the CIP; and
Recognize construction in progress based on progress billings and other acceptable and appropriate supporting documents which will properly substantiate its balance.
The Municipal Engineer to constantly update the Municipal Accountant on the status of accomplishments of on-going Municipality’s projects for accurate presentation of related accounts in the FS.

We recommended that Management:
Submit to the Audit Team copies of the MOA and supporting documents on the subject FTs and ensure their submission for any subsequent FTs; and
Direct the Accountant to:
Review, correct and update the identified schedules with negative balance and conduct periodical check for negative (abnormal) balances in the accounts and adjust accordingly;
Evaluate the non-moving sub-account balances and facilitate the necessary liquidation/refund of unexpended balance of those projects to the SA;
To submit to the concerned SA the liquidation reports and return/remit the outstanding balance, for completed projects, if warranted.
If the disposition of the unspent/unutilized amount is not provided in the MOA, Trust Agreement or Memorandum of Understanding, the agency may, at their option, utilize the said amount by sending a letter to the source agency requesting approval to transfer the unexpended balance to the GF. (Section 3.2 of COA Circular 2012-001); and
Reconcile the Due to NGAs account and take up the necessary correcting/adjusting entries for fair presentation in the FS.

We recommended the Management:
Direct all the concerned officials and employees to settle their CAs within the prescribed period;
Direct the MT to stop indicating as purpose in the DV withdrawal from deposit or other similar purpose but instead indicate the actual CA purpose;
File request for authority to write-off dormant or long outstanding CAs, if necessary, to the Office of the Auditor, pursuant to COA Circular no. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016; and
Direct the Municipal Accountant to:
Refrain from granting additional CA unless the previous CAs are liquidated; and
Issue demand letters to officials and employees, who have outstanding CAs as of December 31, 2023, especially those who are still in the active service with material amount including those individuals who are assigned in other LGUs or other offices. A copy of which must be submitted to the Audit Team with proof of service and dated to enforce settlement of unliquidated CAs.

We recommended that Management:
Require the Municipal Engineer to provide the GSO and the Inventory Committee, if any, with complete description and cost segregation of road components for road projects to facilitate the preparation of the RPCLRN;
Direct the GSO to prepare and submit the RLRN, in accordance with the prescribed formats as provided under COA Circular No. 2015-008; and
Require the Municipal Accountant to disclose the total road network in the Notes to the FS and provide depreciation to all road networks following the provisions of COA Circular No. 2015-008.

We recommended the Management to require the AOs to have all collections as of year-end be remitted and deposited intact and in full amount with the Authorized Government Depository Bank (AGDB). Henceforth, strict adherence to Section 69 of PD No. 1445 and Sections 31 and 32 of Manual on NGAS for LGUs is required.

We recommended that the Agency Head instruct the Municipal Accountant to immediately submit the lacking/unsubmitted CY 2023 TBs, Financial Reports and Statements, DVs with complete supporting documents, collections accounts and other monthly reports as well as the lacking DVs from prior years. Otherwise, the subject disbursements will be disallowed in audit.
Henceforth, require the Municipal Officials concerned to comply with the rules and regulations on the submission of paid DVs, ORs, JEVs, FS and other Financial Reports.

We recommended that Management require from the concerned elected official to liquidate immediately the subject CAs and stop the practice of granting elected officials CAs for purposes other than for official traveling expenses. Instead, designate a SDO to handle/facilitate payment of various operating expenses.

We recommended that Management:
Inform the Audit Team within ten days after the award of the infrastructure project or before the start of the program/activity that the appropriate project signboards and/or public notices are already posted for the validation of the Audit Team; and
Require the Municipal Engineer to provide the Audit Team, at the beginning of the year, with a list of all on-going government PPAs and those that are to be implemented during the year including the required data/information as well as the monthly monitoring report on the Project Status (in Annex “A”) in accordance with Sections 2, 3.1 and 3.2 of COA Circular No. 2013-004.

We recommended that Management:
Direct the GFPS to fully implement the GAD PPAs and monitor their implementation to ensure that optimum benefits are derived by its constituents;
Direct the GFPS and GAD key personnel to undergo capacity building programs and training on gender and development to enhance their knowledge and build their competencies on GAD-related laws and international commitments, gender mainstreaming, gender analysis, gender-responsive planning and budgeting, and GAD-related tools like HGDG on the attributed budget to evaluate gender responsiveness of PPAs in the GAD Plan and Budget; and
Furnish the Audit Team with a copy of the GAD AR within the prescribed period, in compliance with COA Circular No. 2014-001.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to conduct reconciliation, workback, recovery of documents and verification of account balances to facilitate the preparation and maintenance of SLs, preparation of the required Schedule of SL balances and provision of necessary disclosures for all asset, liability and other accounts, where appropriate, to be submitted to the Audit Team together with the year-end FS.

We recommended that Management:
Require the Municipal Accountant and OIC-MT to undertake periodic monitoring of cash accountabilities and ensure that the collections held by the Collecting Officers are within the cash accountability as per approved bond; and
Cause the increase of the fidelity bond of the Liquidating Officer.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the OIC-MT and all AOs to prepare and immediately submit their updated cashbooks to the Office of the Audit Team. Henceforth, prepare, maintain and regularly update their respective cashbooks, as well as, regularly reconcile the same with the accounting records so that any items for adjustment may be corrected and resolved promptly and the amount of their respective accountabilities is accurate and ascertainable at any given time.

We recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
Reconcile the Due to NGAs account, prepare the required SLs of all fund transfers included in the balances of the account under each fund and take up the necessary correcting/adjusting entries in the books for the fair presentation of the said account in the FS; and
Prepare and submit liquidation reports of outstanding fund transfers received from various agencies which purposes were already implemented/completed, return any unspent amount and henceforth, strictly adhere with the provisions of Section 4 of COA Circular No. 94-013 dated December 13, 1994 and Section 3.1.2 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012

We recommended that Management:
Direct the Municipal Engineer and the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator to:
Ensure that tarpaulin signboards for each project, properly labeled and prepared in accordance with the prescribed design and specifications under COA Circular No. 2013-004, are installed or posted at the project site as soon as the award has been made, and the cost of which must be included in the project cost;
Submit at the beginning of the year a list of all on-going government PPAs and those that are to be implemented during the year; and
Instruct the Municipal Accountant to require the attachment of photos of installed project signboards at the project site in processing related payments.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant and other concerned personnel to ensure timely submission of the subsequent FS and Reports, the paid DVs and their supporting documents in compliance with existing rules and regulation.
We recommended that Management require the concerned officials to submit the lacking documents to support the payments for further audit evaluation.

We recommended that Management:
Require all the members of the Inventory Committee to participate and strategize in the conduct of a systematic and complete physical count of PPE in accordance with COA Circular No. 2020-006 and ensure timely submission of RPCPPE, with appropriate information on their conditions, to the Office of the Auditor; and
Require the Municipal Treasurer to maintain Property Cards using the prescribed format in Annex 37 of NGAS for LGUs and the Municipal Accountant to continually maintain Property Ledger Cards per category on all PPE and continuously coordinate with each other to reconcile the discrepancies in their respective PPE books of accounts.

We recommended that Management:
Require the Collecting Officers to remit their collections daily to the Municipal Treasurer, otherwise, impose appropriate actions to compel them to diligently observe their obligation; and
Strict compliance to the provisions of Section 69 of P.D. No. 1445 and Section 26 of COA Circular No. 92-382 must be observed.

We recommended that Local Chief Executive to:
Strictly enforce the submission of report on information and publicity of LGU PPAs as provided in COA Circular No. 2013-004; and
Direct proper coordination and reconciliation of the concerned LGU officials particularly the Municipal Engineer and the Municipal Accountant, in order to facilitate the preparation and submission of the required reports to the COA within due date.

We recommended that Local Chief Executive:
Require the Municipal Accountant to submit the monthly reports and DVs religiously with complete supporting documents within the prescribed period; and
Remind the concerned officials and employees of the administrative or criminal liability, under Section 39 (1) (3) of PD No. 1445 and Article 218 of the Revised Penal Code, as amended by Section 41 of RA No. 10951, which may be imposed as a consequence of their inability or refusal to render accounts without justifiable cause.

We recommended Management to:
Strictly adhere to the provisions of Items 5.1.10 and 5.1.11 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012, and ensure that savings from completed projects of LDRRMF for CO are not transferred to Special Trust Fund; and
Remove from the Special Trust Fund - LDRRMF the balance of unspent LDRRMF for CO amounting to ₱9,204.44 and return the same to the General Fund books. Such savings must be used for DRRM activities and included in LDRRMFIP of the LGU.

We recommend that the Local Chief Executive to:
Require the Municipal Accountant to strictly comply with the documentary requirements provided in COA Circular No. 2023-004 prior to processing the claims;
Submit the lacking documents of the payments for terminal leave benefits with deficient documentation to avoid issuance of Notice of Suspension of such financial transactions; and
Dissemination of the latest COA Circular to retiring officials and employees is advisable, in order for them to promptly prepare the necessary documents and expedite the processing of their claims.

We recommended that Management direct the GFPS to:
Conduct an updated gender sensitivity analysis and implement programs and activities that specifically address gender issues based on the results of the analysis;
Reconsider incorporating the listed PPAs into other funds aside from GAD Fund, such as GF, SEF, and other special purpose fund; and
Submit the attachments of GAD AR as mentioned in Item 4.C.8.5 of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) direct the Inventory Committee take full opportunity to conduct an appropriate and complete one-time cleansing of PPE balances under COA Circular No. 2020-006 and to submit the required ICF and RPCPPE to the Office of the Auditor. We also recommended that the GSO and the Municipal Accountant maintain updated Property Cards and PPELC, respectively, per category on all PPE as recorded in the books of accounts, and continuously coordinate with each other in order to reconcile the discrepancies in the respective PPE accounts.
We further recommended Management to consider creation of additional permanent positions or designation of additional personnel in the Office of the GSO, equipped with appropriate training, who will help the GSO in the effective monitoring of the procurement and issuance of supplies and property, in maintaining updated Property Cards per category on all Agency’s assets as recorded in the list of PPEs of accounting records, and in facilitating the proper conduct of the physical count and preparation of the RPCPPE.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant examine all the entries made for the fund transfers, specifically those that were fully implemented but still outstanding and those unverified balances without any detailed documentation. This examination must include review of the previous years’ records and effecting correcting entries to arrive at the accurate balances to be presented in the FS.
In case that the outstanding balances in the Due to NGAs account are determined to be funds for the completed projects and the remaining balances are no longer needed, we recommended that Management remit them back to the source agencies as required under existing rules and regulations.

We recommended that the LCE direct the Municipal Accountant to give preferential attention on the preparation and submission of the required BRS pursuant to Section 3 of COA Circular No. 96-011, maintain SLs per bank accounts, review the records relevant to the Cash in Bank account and effect necessary adjustments to correct the amount presented in the FS and other documents.

We recommended that Management:
Require the Agency’s Inventory Committee/Appraisal Committee in coordination with the Municipal Agriculturist to verify the existence and ascertain the fair value less cost to sell of the identified Biological Assets, and prepare a report thereon to be submitted to the Office of the Municipal Accountant;
Direct the Municipal Accountant, based on the report to be prepared by the concerned personnel, to prepare adjusting entries to reflect the correct balance of the Breeding Stock account.  Henceforth, Biological Assets, at each reporting date, shall be measured at fair value less cost to sell as required in IPSAS 27; and
Advise the Municipal Agriculturist to regularly furnish the report on the changes in the fair value of the Biological Assets of the LGU to ensure up-to-date fair value measurement of the Biological Assets.

We recommended that Management:
Require the GSO and the Municipal Assessor to exert effort in the retrieval of land titles and other necessary documents as basis to establish ownership and determine proper valuation;
Allocate adequate budget for the titling of the subject land properties in the name of the Municipal Government of Uson to establish absolute ownership and reduce the risk of third-party claims; and
Direct the Municipal Accountant to verify the recorded and unrecorded Land items to properly account them using the cost or fair value and effect the necessary adjustments in the books to arrive at a more reliable Land account balance in accordance with IPSAS No. 17 or any other applicable accounting standards.

We recommended that the LCE:
Direct the Municipal Accountant and GSO to strictly adhere to the provisions of Sections 114 and 121 of the MNGAS for LGUs on the proper recording and reporting of transactions, which requires that all regular purchases of supplies must first be booked through the inventory account and the SSMI is needed as a basis for recording the expenditures using appropriate expenditure accounts; and
Direct the Municipal Accountant and the GSO to maintain the SLC and stock cards for each item of supplies and materials and to monitor and control the status of the stocks on hand.

We recommended that Management:
At the beginning of the year, provide the assigned SA and ATL with a list of all on-going government PPA and those that are to be implemented during the year;
Direct the Project Engineers/BAC to strictly adhere to the provisions of 2.2.6 of COA Circular No. 2013-004 and avoid display of any symbol or graphic representation associated with the top leadership; and
Henceforth, inform the Audit Team within 10 days after the award of the infrastructure project or before the start of the program/activity, that the appropriate project signboards and/or public notices are already posted to facilitate timely validation of the same.

We recommended to require the Municipal GSO to insure with the GIF of GSIS the LGU’s insurable assets and properties with the appropriate property insurance.  We further recommend to submit the Municipality’s duly accomplished PIF to the Office of the Supervising Auditor or the Audit Team Leader and the GIF of GSIS on or before April 30 of each year to be used as basis for the assessment of the general insurance coverage.

We recommended that Management:
Direct the GAD GFPS to strictly observe the timelines in the preparation and submission of GPB and GAD Accomplishment Report pursuant to COA Circular No. 2014-001 dated March 18, 2014 and require the GFPS to submit an updated/revised GAD Plan and Budget to the concerned DILG Office for information and monitoring of the revised PPAs; and
Instruct the GFPS to diligently coordinate with the officials concerned to ensure that the GAD PPAs are optimally, efficiently, and promptly implemented in order to achieve the desired gender-responsiveness development of the Municipality.

We recommended that Management:
Instruct the MDRRM Officer to ensure that only those expenditures which are supportive and attributable to core objectives of DRRM PPAs are charged to the LDRRMF; and
Direct the Municipal Budget Officer to strictly observe the proper charging of expenditures to the 30 percent QRF to ensure that the funds are utilized in accordance with its intended purpose.

We recommended that Management ensure that all disbursements made by the Municipality are supported with complete documentation as prescribed in COA Circular No. 2012-001 and COA Circular No. 2009-001 and to see to it that the attached documents, such as the POs, IARs, etc. are completely and properly signed and accomplished in compliance with the applicable laws and regulations to ascertain the validity and propriety of every transaction made by the Municipality.

We recommended to require the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to transfer the unutilized balance of the LDRRMF from the General Fund to the Trust Fund under the account Trust Liability – DRRM. Ensure that the unexpended balance of QRF and LDRRF – MOOE for each year be timely transferred at year-end with the corresponding cash back up in order to reflect the correct balance of the cash and liability accounts in the FS.

We recommended that Management:
Require the concerned officials to immediately submit the DVs and supporting documents for CY 2023 covering the months of September to December, ORs including the corresponding reports for the months of October to December 2023 and required trial balance and FS;
Require the Office of the Municipal Accountant to install control measures on the observance of the required timelines in the submission of accounts/reports to the Office of the Auditor. Further, require the Office of the Municipal Treasurer to immediately submit the paid vouchers/payrolls accounts and issued ORs to the Office of the Municipal Accountant for recording in the Agency books of accounts, after which, the same must be submitted to the Auditor within the first 10 days of the ensuing month including their supporting documents pursuant to Section 107 of PD No. 1445 and COA Circular No. 2009-006.; and
It is suggested that the Office of the Municipal Treasurer submit the monthly accounts on or before the fifth day of the ensuing month to the Office of the Municipal Accountant to ensure compliance with the required deadlines.

We recommended that Management:
Direct all the concerned officials and employees to settle their CAs within the prescribed period; Direct the Municipal Treasurer to stop indicating as purpose in the DV withdrawal from deposit or other similar purpose but instead indicate the actual CA purpose; File request for authority to write-off dormant or long outstanding CAs, if necessary, to the Office of the Auditor, pursuant to COA Circular No. 2016-005; and Direct the Municipal Accountant to: i) Refrain from granting additional CA unless the previous CAs are liquidated; and ii) Issue demand letters to officials and employees, who have outstanding CAs as of December 31, 2023, especially those who are still in the active service with material amount including those individuals who are assigned in other LGUs or other offices. A copy of which must be submitted to the Audit Team with proof of service and dated to enforce settlement of unliquidated CAs.

We recommended that Management:

Review and evaluate the existing manpower resources to ensure adequate staffing pattern that suit the present needs of the offices and create clear-cut policies and guidelines to address the issue on the necessity and propriety of hiring job orders/holders of COS;

Maximize the use of existing work force and resort to job order hiring only in the exigency of the service and if it is not feasible for the LGU to hire said services under a casual or contractual appointment; and

Require the submission of detailed and quantified accomplishment reports and definite proof of identification to support the payments and require the Disbursing Officer to personally pay at the premises of the Office of the Municipal Treasurer the daily/monthly wage of the job orders/holders of COs especially those assigned in the field or in the barangays for internal control purposes.

Management has evaluated the adequacy of its existing staffing pattern and maximized the use of existing work force. Management has also resorted to accepting summer job applicants or on-the-job training students.

The Accomplishment Reports and contracts for JOs/Laborers paid under SEF were detailed and quantified. Photos were also attached to support the related payments.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management require the Municipal Engineer to properly monitor the timelines of the implementation of infrastructure projects to avoid unnecessary delay, or if unavoidable, to ensure that there is an approved Request for Time Extension, Suspension Order or Variation Order to support the same. Management must also ensure that liquidated damages is imposed and paid by the Contractor in case of unjustified delays.

For purposes of economy, devise measures to ensure that the submission of documents/ reports and other data to COA and other government agencies/financial institutions are consolidated and submitted only once a week, unless urgent and due for submission; and

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct all end-user units of the Agency to prepare the PPMP, incorporating therein all their procurement requirements for supplies, materials and PPE during the year. This must be duly evaluated and reviewed by the Municipal Budget Officer and be submitted to the BAC Secretariat for consolidation into a comprehensive, workable and realistic APP for approval by the HoPE as basis for procurement.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant require all future procurements for supplies, materials and PPEs be supported by an approved PPMP/APP, pursuant to the appropriate documentary requirements provided in COA Circular 2012-001.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant strictly observe/comply with the timeline for the submission of Financial Reports especially the Subsidiary Ledgers.

Devise Accounting procedures/methods to cope up with the submission of Accounting Reports to COA as well as other Financial Reports required by other agencies. observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that Management require the City Accounting Office and the Inventory Committee to prioritize the reconciliation process of the PPE and Biological Assets accounts and ensure that discrepancies noted are immediately investigated and adjusted in the books accordingly.
We also recommended that Management require thorough verification of the recorded PPEs without details in order to determine their particulars and/or their true status, and to make the necessary adjustments in the books of accounts of the City Government based on the result of their verification.

We recommended that the City Accountant thoroughly review the identified reconciling items and immediately prepare the necessary journal entries to take it up in the books of the LGU so that the Cash in Bank account will be fairly presented in the financial statements.

We recommended to require the City Accountant, the City Treasurer and the implementing departments to continuously exert effort not only to locate the supporting documents of the outstanding Receivables but also to document all procedures employed to establish the location or whereabouts of the debtors and borrowers involved including the actions taken to collect the amounts due from them.
We further recommended that if all possible actions and efforts to collect were exhausted but still no progress of collections is noted, then the City Accountant and the City Treasurer must seek approval from the concerned City Officials for the appropriate remedial measures to rid the books of invalid receivables in order to fairly present the Receivable accounts in the City’s financial statements.

We recommended that the City Accountant make the necessary adjusting entries to record the set-up of SET Receivable in order to properly present the correct balances of the affected accounts in the financial statements.
We further recommended that subsidiary ledgers for SET Receivables must be regularly updated so that verification of the correctness of the balances of these accounts could be readily made.
Henceforth, Management must strictly comply with Section 20 of the MNGAS, Volume I on setting up of SET Receivable.  The City Treasurer must prepare the Certified List of Taxpayers and submit it to the City Accountant.  Based on this, the City Accountant must take up in the books, the SET Receivable and maintain the corresponding subsidiary ledgers to monitor tax collections and delinquencies.

We recommended and reiterated the following:
Cause for the immediate liquidation of the outstanding cash advances amounting to ₱1,552,315.42 as of December 31, 2021 to reflect the correct balances of the accounts affected in the books of accounts and submit the liquidation report or proof of refund/s, if there are any, to the Auditor for post-audit;
Send demand letters to the concerned AOs, with unliquidated cash advances for monitoring purposes
Henceforth, avoid granting subsequent cash advances to AOs with outstanding cash advances and withhold the salaries and/or any amount due those AOs who were unable to comply with the pertinent rules and regulations on cash advances in accordance with the afore-cited law and regulations; and
Adhere strictly to the guidelines in the granting, utilization and settlement of cash advances as enunciated in COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997.

We recommended that Management require the Liquidating Officers to thoroughly verify the RCD of the Collecting Officers.  Henceforth, the prescribed guidelines on collections and deposit per Sections 31 and 35 of the MNGAS must be strictly followed.

We recommended that Management require the Revenue Collectors to prepare the RAAF and the City Treasurer to prepare the CRAAF in the prescribed form provided in the NGAS Manual and submit the same not later than the 5th day of the ensuing month to the Audit Team.  Strict adherence to Sections 50 and 51 of the MNGAS is required.

We recommended that Management require the City Accountant to record the necessary adjusting entries in order to correct the classification of the affected accounts in the financial statements.  Henceforth, the provisions of COA Circular No. 2015-009 must be strictly observed in order to ensure the fair presentation of accounts in the financial statements.

We recommended that Management require the City Accountant to ensure that withholding taxes are deducted from payments made in accordance with the Revised Withholding Tax Table and the same are remitted to the BIR within the prescribed period.
We further recommended that Management require the BAC, through its Secretariat, to submit the necessary documentary requirements to support honoraria claims.  Also, the City Accountant must ensure the completeness of supporting documents before approving any disbursement voucher for payment.
Henceforth, we recommended that the City Treasurer treat collections from funding sources identified in the aforecited DBM Circular as trust receipts to make sure that the fund will be fully utilized for the purpose.

We recommended that Management enhance close monitoring activities on fund transfer and to direct the Focal Person and Project Proponent Department Head to implement immediately without delay the funded programs of all on-going/not started projects to attain the program’s objectives.
We also recommended that Management direct the City Treasurer and City Accountant to remit collections intended for National Agencies within the reglementary period so they will not be deprived of the benefits of its use.
We further recommended that Management direct the City Accountant to thoroughly review the balances of Due to NGAs account and to make the necessary adjusting entries in order to properly present this account in the financial statements.  Also, the City Accountant must return/remit any unexpended amount of fund transfer after project completion pursuant to the aforementioned circulars.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the Bids and Awards Committee to comply with the posting requirements of the aforementioned IRR of RA No. 9184 in order to promote transparency in the utilization of the COVID-19 funds.  We further recommended that the City Government strictly adhere to the guidelines of the procurement law in the proper planning of all future procurements so that the LGU can ensure economical spending of government funds.

We recommended that Management require the submission of warranty security in the form of a retention money or bank guarantee from the supplier. Henceforth, strict adherence to the aforementioned regulations must be enforced in all procurement transactions in order to prevent any government loss and/or audit suspensions.

We recommended the following:
Ensure that the GAD AR is based and consistent with the DILG-endorsed and/or LGU-approved GPB and any deviations thereof as well as the reasons and factors that have facilitated or hindered the implementation of the identified gender issues and activities are cited in the report; and
Submit the duly accomplished GeRL Assessment Tool to the Audit Team to validate that the identified gender issues in the GPB are the results of the assessment of the extent of the LGU’s gender responsiveness as an organization and in the delivery of basic services in order to achieve gender responsive local governance.

We recommended that the Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management Officer coordinate with the City Accountant and City Budget Officer to determine the amount of unexpended balance of the LDRRMF and prepare the LDRRMFIP incorporating the PPAs to be funded from the previous years’ unexpended balances.  Henceforth, the aforementioned provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-002 must be strictly implemented for the speedy realization of the LDRRMF objectives.

We recommended that Management require the concerned City officials to make necessary adjustments in the books of the Agency with regards to the aforementioned errors in accounting and recording of LDRRMF balances.  Strict compliance with COA Circular No. 2012-002 is required.
We also recommended that Management require the City Accountant and City Treasurer to transfer the unutilized balance of the LDRRMF from the General Fund to the Trust Fund under the account Trust Liability-DRRM.  Ensure that the unexpended balance of QRF and LDRRMF-MOOE for each year be timely transferred at year-end with the corresponding cash back up in order to reflect the correct balance of the cash and liability accounts in the financial statements.

We reiterated our previous audit recommendations that Management require the GSO to submit immediately the documents supporting the issuance of the various inventories held for distribution and consumption to the Provincial Accounting Office and the latter to immediately draw adjusting entries for the fair presentation of FS of the Provincial Government of Masbate.
We further recommended that Management establish an effective and efficient internal control on supply and property management, specifically on the: a) centralization of custody, handling and issuance of supplies and materials in the GSO; b) preparation and regular submission of the SSMI to the Provincial Accountant for the timely recognition of expenses; c) complete conduct of the required physical count of all supplies and properties; d) submission of the required RPCI semestrally in accordance with the afore-cited rules and regulations; and e) maintenance of complete and updated stock cards and subsidiary ledger cards by the GSO and Accounting Office, respectively.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Local Chief Executive require the Office of the Provincial Accountant to:
Submit to the Auditor the supporting documents of all accumulated APs as reflected in the  Schedule  of  AP  as  of  December 31, 2021   to  back  up  the  authenticity of the recorded obligations in the books of accounts of the Agency;
Undertake in-depth evaluation to identify the veracity, nature and claim of the recorded payables both for the current and prior years ledgers/files to determine which are valid claims or fictitious accounts; and
Take up the appropriate reversion/adjustments in the books of the LGU for those established transactions with no valid claimants and with dormant accounts in order to have a fair presentation of accounts in the FS.

We recommended that Management require:
The OIC-Provincial Engineer and Acting Provincial General Services Officer to prepare the turnover documents of the foregoing projects and coordinate with the Property Officer of the DepEd for the acceptance of the school buildings; and
The Provincial Accounting Office to draw a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) in the GF and SEF to derecognize the School Building account from the books and furnish the Accountant of DepEd Masbate with the JEV and its supporting documents as reference in recording in their books. We recommended the following journal entry:

We recommended that Management require:
The Provincial Accountant to properly account all the land properties of the Province by gathering relevant supporting documents from the Provincial Assessor’s Office and effect the necessary adjustments in the books to arrive at a more reliable Land account balance in the FS in accordance with IPSAS 17 and other applicable accounting standards;
The Inventory Committee to undertake an actual count of land based on the updated records from the Provincial Assessor’s Office; and
The Provincial Accountant, General Service Officer and the Provincial Assessor to reconcile their records pertaining to the Land account.

We recommended that Management require:
The Provincial Assessor’s Office to hasten the titling of Land in the name of the Province and register the same with the Registry of Deeds to ensure absolute ownership thereof;
The Provincial Budget Officer to allocate funds for the titling of land in the name of the Province; and
The Provincial Accountant to disclose in the notes to FS the subject 251 parcels of Land and its status of acquisition whether purchased or acquired through donation or transfer without cost until the amounts to be booked-up have been determined.

We recommended that Management require all collectors to prepare RCDs and remit all collections to the LO at the close of each business day in accordance with Section 29 of Manual on NGAS, Vol. I. In case of collectors assigned to the field, where travel time from their places of assignment to the Treasurer’s Office is more than one day, turnover of collections shall be made at least once a week or as soon as the collection reach ₱5,000.00 as prescribed under Section 26 of COA Circular No. 92-382.

We recommended that Management direct the Provincial Accountant, or whoever is in custody thereof, to submit the soonest time to the Office of the Auditor the unsubmitted paid disbursement vouchers, payrolls and liquidation report with complete supporting documents.

We recommended that the Provincial Accountant strictly comply with the rules and regulations on the submission of financial reports. Also, Management must institute appropriate remedial measures to ensure that the required financial reports are submitted promptly without the need to demand from time to time.

We recommended that Management require the OIC, Provincial Treasurer, OIC, Provincial Engineer and Provincial Accountant to immediately submit the disbursement accounts and its supporting documents relative to the above-mentioned projects.

We recommended that Management require:
The OIC-Provincial General Services Officer to comply with the requirements of COA Circular No. 2014-002 in the preparation and maintenance of inventory list of procured items based on the updated stock cards and property cards;
The Provincial Accountant to maintain a separate SL for 30% QRF and 70% DRRMF, by year; and to ensure that all required disclosure in the Notes to FS on inventories and PPE are complied with; and
The OIC-PDRRMO to prepare and submit the monthly Report on the Sources and Utilization of LDRRMF, Report on the Receipt and Utilization of Cash Donations and other reports using formats provided and as prescribed in the aforementioned circulars.

We recommended that Management ensure that the transfer of the unexpended/unobligated balance of the QRF and DRRMF-MOOE of the LDRRMF to the Special Trust Fund in the Trust Fund books must be back-up with actual fund transfer of cash at the end of the year, in order to present accurate balances of accounts in the FS.

We recommended that Management:
Require the Provincial Accounting Office to record financial transactions charged against PCF in accordance with Volume I, Chapter 5 of the Manual on NGAS for LGUs in order to reflect in the Books of Accounts and Financial Reports for Trust Fund accurate financial information;
Require the BAC to include in the APP all items necessary for the operation of the nine hospitals and ensure that no procurement will be undertaken unless it is in accordance with the APP in accordance with Section 7.2 of the Revised IRR of R.A. 9184; and
Submit explanation why the expenditures charged against PCF were not considered/included in the APP.

We recommended that Management require the Provincial officials and employees concerned to:
Submit copies of the MOA on fund transfers from NGAs during CY 2021 and effect the submission thereon whenever future fund transfers are involved; and
Comply with the mandatory submission of perfected contracts, purchase orders and its supporting documents to COA within the period prescribed by COA Circular No. 2009-001 for a timely and complete conduct of its auditorial and technical review.

We recommended that Management require:
Consultants to pay to the BIR the amount of withholding tax and then submit proofs of payment to the auditor, otherwise, it will be disallowed in audit; and
The officials concerned compliance with the above-cited laws and regulations on withholding and remittance of taxes from payments for consultancy services.

We recommended that Management:
Submit immediately the lacking documentary requirements and justification on inconsistencies with the provisions of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184, otherwise, the financial transactions will be subject to either suspension or disallowance in audit;
Submit BAC Resolutions and Minutes of Meetings pertaining to the said procurement of medicines and medical supplies; and
Ensure that the procurement of medicines and medical supplies conforms with existing government laws, rules and regulations.

We recommended that Management must notify the public and Auditor through posting of relevant information of the projects on tarpaulin signboards in the place where the project is located or carried out within ten days after the award of the infrastructure project regardless of amount or source of funds.  Adhere strictly to the guidelines stated under COA Circular No. 2013-004.

We recommended that Management require the GSO to submit to the Audit Team copies of perfected contracts/POs with complete supporting documents within five working days from the issuance thereof for timely and complete conduct of auditorial and technical review.
Also, informed the Office of the Auditor within 24 hours upon acceptance of deliveries so that timely inspection will be made to ascertain the completeness of the items and conformity with specifications.

We recommended that Management instruct the Provincial Program Management and Implementing Unit (PPMIU) to assist the PG in the preparation and submission of financial reports for them to comply with the reportorial requirements of monitoring bodies under PRDP.

We recommended that Management:
Instruct the PPMIU assigned to institute preventive measures and regular monitoring of animals to minimize government losses brought about by mortality due to diseases which could have otherwise been prevented;
Determine accountability and culpability on the death of 123 does and five bucks and the loss of ₱958,800.00 and take appropriate actions, as warranted; and
Closely monitor and enforce compliance by all stakeholders of their respective responsibilities and obligations.

We commended the PG for its compliance on the submission of GAD Plan and Budget for CY 2021 as well as the AR to the DILG.  We recommended, however, that the GAD Focal Point System/Focal Person prepare and submit the other required reports to be attached to the GAD AR.

We recommended Management to require the settlement of the suspensions and disallowances by the persons determined to be liable pursuant to Section 9 of COA Circular No. 2009-006 and to notify the accountable persons who are not currently employed by the Agency to settle their obligations.

Instruct the City Accountant to exert effort in determining the cause of the negative balance/cash overage including obtaining explanation from the concerned AOs. If the overage can’t be satisfactory explained by the AOs, prepare adjusting entry to credit the Miscellaneous Income account in order to eliminate the negative balance of the Cash in Treasury Account in the Financial Statements.

We recommended that the City Accountant thoroughly review the identified reconciling items and immediately prepare the necessary journal entries to take it up in the books of the LGU.  We further recommended that the City Accountant exert effort to reconcile the variance in the balances between the GL and the Bank Statements amounting to ₱23.77 million. Henceforth, ensure that the monthly BRS is prepared and submitted within the required time frame to facilitate early detection and correction of errors so that the Cash in Bank account will be fairly presented in the financial statements.

We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that Management require the City Accounting Office and the Inventory Committee, to prioritize the reconciliation process of the PPE and Biological Assets accounts and ensure that discrepancies noted are immediately investigated and adjusted in the books accordingly.
We also recommended that Management require thorough verification of the recorded PPEs without details in order to determine their particulars and/or their true status, and to make the necessary adjustments in the books of accounts of the City Government based on the result of their verification.

We recommended that the City Accountant determine if the specific programs/projects for which the funds were intended were already implemented, goods/services were already delivered, and liquidation of funds were already submitted and received by the source agencies.  We further recommended that the necessary adjusting entries be made in order to present the correct balances of the affected accounts in the financial statement of the City of Masbate. Henceforth, the Accounting Division must ensure that all fund transfers are properly taken up in the books and that proper accounting and reporting is made of the utilization of the funds.

We recommended that the City Accountant and other City Officials concerned submit the lacking supporting documents enumerated in Annex C and henceforth ensure the completeness of supporting documents before approving any disbursement voucher for payment in order to ascertain the legality and validity of all claims against the funds of the LGU.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the Local Finance Committee to comply with the reporting and posting requirements of the aforementioned LBC in order to promote transparency in the utilization of the Bayanihan Grant.

We recommended that Management strictly adhere to the above cited provisions of COA Circular No. 2009-001.  Henceforth, all contracts together with its supporting documents must be submitted for review and evaluation to the Office of the Auditor within five working days from execution.

We recommended that Management require the City Accountant to:
Review and analyze the reconciling items of reciprocal accounts and take-up necessary adjustments in the respective books of accounts;
Prepare a reconciliation report of reciprocal accounts to ensure that all reconciling items are resolved and/or adjusted; and
Perform constant review and appraisal of transactions to deter errors and omissions.
We also recommended that the concerned personnel must be cautious in the recording of the transactions, keeping in mind that relative to the intra-agency transfer of funds, what has been debited in one fund must have a corresponding credit in the other fund for the same amount.

We recommended that the provisions of Section 3 of the PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2016-01 be complied with by subjecting the GAD PPAs to the GAD/HGDG Checklist during the project identification and designing programs to determine whether the same fully address the gender issues and concerns of the locality.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Office of the City Accountant to install control measures on the observance of the required timelines in the submission of accounts/reports to the Office of the Auditor particularly on the General Fund-Proper accounts. Further, require the Office of the City Treasurer to immediately submit the paid vouchers/payrolls accounts and issued ORs to the Office of the City Accountant for recording in the Agency books of accounts, after which, the same must be submitted to the Auditor within the first 10 days of the ensuing month including their supporting documents pursuant to Section 107 of PD No. 1445 and COA Circular No. 2009-006.
It was suggested that the Office of the City Treasurer submit the monthly accounts on or before the fifth day of the ensuing month to the Office of the City Accountant to ensure compliance with the required deadlines.
However, we recommended that additional effort be exerted to implement the remaining unimplemented projects so that the intended beneficiaries can enjoy the benefits that can be derived therefrom.

Further, require the Office of the City Treasurer to immediately submit the paid vouchers/payrolls accounts and issued ORs to the Office of the City Accountant for recording in the Agency books of accounts, after which, the same must be submitted to the Auditor within the first ten (10) days of the ensuing month including their supporting documents pursuant to Section 107 of P.D. 1445 and COA Circular 2009-006.

It was suggested that the Office of the City Treasurer submit the monthly accounts on or before the fifth day of the ensuing month to the Office of the City Accountant to ensure compliance with the required deadlines.

Moreover, we reiterated the compliance on the submission of the Accountant’s Advice for all fund disbursements of the Agency. Prompt submission of accounts and other mandated reports will facilitate timely review and verification of the Agency’s financial transactions, thus, corrective measures can be implemented, if necessary.
observations.managementAction: Advised the Acting City Treasurer to submit the paid vouchers/payrolls and issued ORs to the Accounting Office within the prescribed period. Required both Offices to comply the regulations relative to the submission of monthly reports and accounts.  The Agency was able to submit the year-end financial reports for CY 2019 on March 27, 2020.

Restated finding- Please see related Audit Observation No.  2.

The concerned Offices was still unable to submit the required monthly accounts and reports within the prescribed period.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated the previous year’s audit recommendations that the concerned Agency officials must strictly abide to the above-cited guidelines on cash advances. Also, require the City Accountant to stop the practice of granting cash advances to officials and employees who have outstanding balanced. The City Accountant must see to it that all outstanding balance of cash advances are fully liquidated every end of the year and ensure that the corresponding liquidations are recorded to their appropriate accounts in order to present fairly the assets and expenses accounts of the city.

Cause for the immediate liquidation of the outstanding cash advances amounting to Ᵽ4.98 million as of December 31, 2017 and submit the liquidation report to the Auditor for post-audit. Send Demand Letters to the concerned Accountable Officer(s) with unliquidated cash advances. Appropriate legal action against those erring officials/employees must be initiated for continued non-compliance with the existing laws and regulations, if warranted.
observations.reason: Restated finding- Please see related Audit Observation No.  1.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant to make the necessary adjustments in the books pertaining to the understatement of the set-up of SET Receivables for CY 2016 and 2017. Likewise, instruct the City Assessor and the Officer -in-Charge (OIC), City Treasurer to immediately submit the complete Assessment Roll and RPTAR to the City Accountant in order to facilitate the setting up of RPT ad SET receivables account. Likewise, collectibles for the prior year’s and collections thereto must be kept updated through the maintenance of subsidiary ledger cards to ensure the reliability of the account balance of Real Property Tax and Special Education Tax Receivables and the corresponding Liability Accounts under Deferred Income Accounts.

We recommended that Management require the concerned LGU personnel to immediately prepare and submit the liquidation reports with complete supporting documents relative to the utilization of trust funds received from these National Government Agencies. It must be assured that proper reporting and accounting is made on the utilization of the fund transfers to fairly present financial transactions at year-end. See to it that reconciliation of the records between the LGU as Implementing Agency and the Source Agencies be undertaken and appropriate adjustments/corrections, if any, be made in the books. Require the City Accountant to trace out the specific account for the balance of ₱6,735,882.88 without the supporting schedules.

For the excess funds from completed projects, we recommended that these must be returned to the Source Agency in compliance with the guidelines of COA Circular No. 94-013. Require the officials concerned the immediate implementation of priority projects/programs given by National Government Agencies.
observations.managementAction: Refund of balances was remitted to concerned agencies. Further, the City Accounting Office still in the process of tracing out the records on prior year’s balance without schedule.
observations.reason: There are still documents that are in process and funds for utilization.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the CDRRMO to submit an updated City Disaster Risk Reduction Management Fund Investment Plan (CDRRMFIP) as recommended by the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Council (LDRRMC) showing the programs, projects and activities that must be incorporated in the local development plan and annual work and financial plan of the city government as well as the inclusion on the use of QRF. The City Budget Officer (CBO) must see to it that in the preparation of the budget proposal for the ensuing year, the approved LRRMFIP be the basis in making the current year appropriations for QRF, MF-MOOE and MF-CO.

In addition, submit the Sangguniang Panlungsod Resolution adopting the declaration of calamity in Masbate City as declared by the Sangguniang Panlalawigan per Resolution No. 167-2017 declaring the Province of Masbate under the state of calamity caused by Tropical Depression “Urduja” and authority to utilize the 30% QRF for CY 2017.

Also, the CDRRMC must revisit the allocation/utilization of the calamity fund, particularly the Quick Response Fund, in order to have the allocation of funds proportionate to the extent of damage of the calamity that nay hit the area of jurisdiction of the city government. Henceforth, strict adherence to COA Circular No. 2012-002 is enjoined.

Considering the longer period of time that has elapsed which is almost six months from the targeted date of its completion on October 7, 2017, it is suggested that Management undertake speedy resolution to cause the immediate completion of the project. Otherwise, enforce appropriate legal action against the contractor for damages, if warranted.

We recommended, that the GAD Focal Point System ensure that:
The DILG, Regional Office issue the Certificate of Endorsement on the submitted LGU GAD Plan and Budget (GPB) and Review of GAD Accomplishment Report (GAD AR);
GAD Database be developed and maintained as well as regularly updated as sources of relevant information for a systematic planning, programming and policy formulation in order to address the priority gender issues; and
Gender analysis be conducted on the PPAs implemented to determine which among them are responsive to the identified gender issues; and
Monitoring and evaluation system be established to         periodically assess the GAD related accomplishments and significant concerns.

We recommended that Management strictly adhere to Section 7, 8 and 9 of COA Circular No. 2009-001 governing the rendition of accounts and submission of copies of contracts. Direct the BAC Secretariat, City Engineer and City Planning and Development Coordinator to closely coordinate with one another in the compliance of the required documentation and submission to the Audit Team. Further, it is suggested that the Office of the City Accountant require the attachment of complete supporting documents on the duly processed disbursements vouchers pertaining to infrastructure projects before effecting the payment.
observations.managementAction: (See related Audit Observation No. 12)

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management consider the creation of special account for economic enterprise for its market operation and require the Office of the City Accountant, in close coordination with the Economic Enterprise Manager, the preparation of the corresponding financial reports separate from the General Fund-Proper in compliance with Section 313 of Republic Act No. 7160 and Section 105, Chapter 6, Vol. I of the New Government Accounting System (NGAS) for LGUs. This will serve as an effective tool in strategy formulation and decision-making activities of the senior management.

Moreover, pass a resolution through the Sanggunian Panlungsod authorizing the LGU for the opening of a separate bank account for Market Operations of Masbate City.

We recommended that Management require the previous ICO, City Treasurer and Collectors to immediately deposit/remit the balance of ₱3,665,441.66 in the ensuing year. Also, require all accountable Officers to desist from handling huge amount of cash in their possession and deposit all their collections for personal or other purposes. Further, initiate appropriate disciplinary action against those erring accountable officers for continued and/or deliberate non-compliance with the existing laws and regulations, if warranted.

We recommended that management (a) convene the Inventory Committee and fast track the conduct of physical count, report preparation, and the reconciliation of the inventory results with the ledgers; (b) require the General Services Officer to maintain records on all properties of the agency; and (c) require the City Accountant to properly maintain the subsidiary records of all PPE accounts indicating the complete information and analyze and determine the composition of beginning balances of some PPE categories without the supporting schedules.

Strict adherence to existing laws, auditing rules and regulations in the proper management of government properties is required.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Office of the City Accountant to submit to the Auditor the supporting documents together with the schedule of all accumulated accounts payable as of December 31, 2016 to back up the authenticity of the recorded obligations in the books of accounts of the city government. We suggested that in depth evaluation be undertaken to identify the veracity, nature and claim of the recorded payables for the current year, with emphasis on the prior year’s ledgers/files to determine which are valid or fictitious accounts, Further, for those established transactions with no valid claimants and dormant accounts, the City Accountant must take up the appropriate reversion/adjustments in the books of the LGU for fair presentation of accounts in the financial statements.
observations.reason: Restated finding- Please see related Audit Observation No. 6.

observations.recommendation: Considering that the City has the available cash in bank balance, we recommended requiring the ICO, City Treasurer to immediately remit or deposit the withheld collections due to BIR, GSIS, PhilHEALTH and Pag-IBIG. Require the City Accountant and ICO, City Treasurer to prepare the schedule of monthly remittances and disbursement vouchers and immediately prepare and release the checks to them. Prompt remittances of these trust liabilities must be done to avoid possible misuse of cash resources and incurrence of unnecessary penalties that may be imposed by the concerned agencies. Further, it is reminded that payments covering the said penalties and surcharges on the late remittances must not be charged to the government funds but instead a personal liability of the concerned officials who failed to comply with the mandated laws and regulations.
observations.managementAction: The balance of unremitted Trust   Receipts has been reduced as of year-end. The Accounting Office has been conducting further verification on prior year’s accounts.
observations.reason: Inability of the Agency officials to observe the required timelines for the release of the trust liabilities payable to the concerned GOCCs & Other Government Agencies.  As of December 31, 2019, the balance of unremitted trust receipts to BIR, GSIS, and Philhealth amounted to ₱5,928,847.50.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the City Accountant to trace out the entries made pertaining to the above-mentioned disbursements and appropriate adjusting entries be undertaken in the books. Strict adherence to the guidelines in the accounting and obligations in the disbursement of local fund is required.

observations.reason: We noted that in the calendar year 2019, the agency still made charges to Prior Period Adjustment (PYA) account for CY 2019 expenditures due to non-recording of accounts payable.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the City Accountant to submit the schedule/details of the computation for the monthly depreciation expense of the property, plant and equipment in order to check the correct balance of the account as presented in the year-end financial statements. Strict adherence to the accounting policies and procedures relative to the costing of property, plant and equipment is required.
